title: Spelt loaf
subtitle: Nutty, slightly sweet spelt sandwich loaf with a soft crumb
excerpt: A spelt loaf: a blend of wholegrain spelt flour and strong bread flour, leavened with yeast, sweetened lightly with honey, and baked in a tin. The crumb is slightly denser than white bread and has a mild, nutty flavour. Spelt's lower gluten strength calls for gentler handling than standard bread dough.
sourceNotes: Spelt (Triticum spelta) is an ancient wheat relative cultivated for thousands of years in Europe. Spelt bread appears in European domestic baking guides from the late nineteenth century and has been widely adopted in modern British domestic baking. The yeast-leavened tin loaf using a blend of spelt and strong white flour is documented in twentieth-century British baking references as an accessible entry to alternative-grain baking and is fully public-domain.

content.text: Spelt loaf is a straightforward tin bread with two points of difference from white bread: a shorter kneading time and a slightly wetter, more relaxed dough. The flavour payoff is worth it: nutty, slightly sweet, with more character than plain white.
Mix and knead, bulk ferment 75 minutes

Mix the {{spelt-flour}}, {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Dissolve the {{honey}} in 340 ml warm water. Add the honey water and {{olive-oil}} to the flour and mix to a shaggy dough. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes. See the
: stop when the dough is smooth but still slightly tacky. Shape into a ball, cover, and bulk ferment for 75 minutes until doubled.

Grease a 900 g loaf tin. Shape the dough into a log and place seam-side-down in the tin. Cover and proof for 45 minutes until risen above the rim. Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Score the top with a single lengthways slash. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until deep gold and hollow-sounding when tapped on the base. The internal temperature should reach 94 to 96°C. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before slicing.
